coredata.dc:title: Polypyrrole and graphene quantum dots @ Prussian Blue hybrid film on graphite felt electrodes: Application for amperometric determination of l-cysteine
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Lei
Tricard, Simon
Yue, Pengwei
Zhao, Jihua
Fang, Jian
Shen, Weiguo
coredata.dc:description: A novel polypyrrole (PPy) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) @ Prussian Blue (PB) nanocomposite has been grafted on a graphite felt (GF) substrate (PPy/GQDs@PB/GF), and has been proven to be an efficient electrochemical sensor for the determination of l-cysteine (l-cys). GQDs, which were fabricated by carbonization of citric acid and adsorbed on GF surface ultrasonically, played an important role for promoting the synthesis process of PB via a spontaneous redox reaction between Fe3+ and [Fe(CN)6]3−. The PPy film has been electro-polymerized to improve the electrochemical stability of the PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode. The as-prepared electrode was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), infrared spectroscopy (IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and electrochemical methods. It exhibited an excellent activity for the electrocatalytic oxidation of l-cys, with a detection sensitivity equal to 0.41Amol−1 L for a concentration range of 0.2–50μmolL−1, and equal to 0.15Amol−1 L for a concentration range of 50–1000μmolL−1. A low detection limit of 0.15μmolL−1, as well as a remarkable long-time stability and a negligible sensitivity to interfering analytes, were also ascertained.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Prussian Blue
Polypyrrole
Biosensors
L-cysteine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM pictures of GQDs/GF (A), GF (inset A), GQDs@PB/GF (B), PB/GF (inset B), PPy/GQDs@PB/GF (D), PPy/GF (inset D). TEM of GQDs@PB/GF (C). Evolution of the UV–vis absorption spectra of the PB precursor solution with addition of GQDs (Sample 1) and without addition of GQDs (Sample 2), (the spectra were recorded 1min, 49min, 67min and 120min after the beginning of the experiment. GQDs were added after 60 min). Inset: absorbance measurement at 710nm as a function of time (E). FTIR spectrum of GQDs@PB/GF (trace a), PPy/GQDs@PB/GF (trace b) and GF (trace c). Inset: enlarged view of trace b (F). XRD patterns of GQDs@PB/GF (a), PPy/GQDs@PB/GF (b) and GF (c) (G) (inset: enlargement of the XRD patterns).

CVs at a GQDs@PB/GF (a), a PPy/GQDs@PB/GF (b) and a PB/GF (c) electrode at a scan rate of 50mVs−1 in a 0.5molL−1 KCl solution (A). CVs of a PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode during the first 100 cycles at a scan rate of 50mVs−1 (B). CVs of a PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode at different scan rates (C). Plot of the anodic current versus the square root of scan rates (R 2=0.994) and cathodic peak current versus the square root of scan rates (R 2=0.997) from 20 to 1000mVs−1 (D). Plot of the oxidation and redox peak potentials as function of the logarithm of the scan rates (E).

CVs of a PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode in the absence (trace a) and presence (trace b) of 1000µmolL−1 L-cys recorded in a 0.5molL−1 KCl solution (A). Chronoamperometric responses of a PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode to successive 2µL addition of L-cys solutions with increasing concentrations at 0.22V (B) (the concentrations in the graph are the concentration in L-cys of the injected solution). Calibration curve obtained from the chronoamperogram with a PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode (C) (y(µA)=−0.41⋅x(μmolL−1)–1.58, R 2=0.975 in the L-cys concentration range of 0.2–50μmolL−1; y(µA)=−0.15⋅x(μmolL−1)–17.73, R 2=0.997 in the L-cys concentration range of 50–1000μmolL−1). Error bars represent standard deviation, n=3.

Influence of addition of some important biomolecules on the determination of 2µL of a 10µmolL−1 L-cys solution with a PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode: L-methionine (A), DL-methionine (B), L-cystine (C), glutathione (D), N-acety-L-cysteine (E), DL-cysteine (F), glucose (G), D-fructose (H), uric acid (I), glycine (J), L-proline (K), phenylalanine (L). The interferents are added at the same concentration (10µmolL−1) except for glutathione (2µmolL−1). The response is normalized with respect to the response of L-cys. Error bars represent standard deviation, n=3.
Comparative data for electroanalysis of L-cys based on some modified electrodes.
Detection and recovery of L-cys in sample dosage forms at a PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode.

head.ce:title: Polypyrrole and graphene quantum dots @ Prussian Blue hybrid film on graphite felt electrodes: Application for amperometric determination of l-cysteine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratoire de Physique et Chimie de Nano-Objets, INSA, CNRS, Université de Toulouse, 135 Avenue de Rangueil, 31077 Toulouse, France

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratoire de Physique et Chimie de Nano-Objets, INSA, CNRS, Université de Toulouse

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel polypyrrole (PPy) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) @ Prussian Blue (PB) nanocomposite has been grafted on a graphite felt (GF) substrate (PPy/GQDs@PB/GF), and has been proven to be an efficient electrochemical sensor for the determination of l-cysteine (l-cys). GQDs, which were fabricated by carbonization of citric acid and adsorbed on GF surface ultrasonically, played an important role for promoting the synthesis process of PB via a spontaneous redox reaction between Fe3+ and [Fe(CN)6]3−. The PPy film has been electro-polymerized to improve the electrochemical stability of the PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode. The as-prepared electrode was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), infrared spectroscopy (IR), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and electrochemical methods. It exhibited an excellent activity for the electrocatalytic oxidation of l-cys, with a detection sensitivity equal to 0.41Amol−1 L for a concentration range of 0.2–50μmolL−1, and equal to 0.15Amol−1 L for a concentration range of 50–1000μmolL−1. A low detection limit of 0.15μmolL−1, as well as a remarkable long-time stability and a negligible sensitivity to interfering analytes, were also ascertained.

ce:section.ce:para: l-cysteine (l-cys) is a sulfur-containing amino acid present in natural proteins. It can act as a cancer indicator, a radio protective agent, an antitoxin, an antioxidant and as a free radical scavenger, and thus plays a crucial role in biological systems (Hou et al., 2015; Pandey et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2005). It is widely present in medicines, food and biological tissues. The abnormal level of l-cys is associated with many diseases, such as slow growth, hair depigmentation, edema, lethargy, liver damage, muscle and fat loss, skin lesions and weakness (Shahrokhian, 2001). Hence, sensitive and selective detection of cysteine is of great importance in many fields such as biological, medical and clinical diagnoses.

In the recent years, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has become an emerging class of the nanocarbon family, thanks to its low toxicity, its easy preparation, its high chemical stability, its environmental friendliness, its luminescence or its ability to transfer photoinduced electron (Miao et al., 2015; Zhao et al., 2015). It has shown great promise applications in the fields of bioimaging (Demchenko and Dekaliuk, 2013; Fan et al., 2014), photoluminescence (Shi et al., 2015), photovoltaic devices (Li et al., 2011; Yan et al., 2010), catalysts (Favaro et al., 2015), and fluorescent sensor (Dong et al., 2012; Lou et al., 2015). In addition, GQDs have been recognized as both excellent electron donors and acceptor, making them interesting candidates for producing electrode materials (Yang et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2015). GQDs can be functionalized (Li et al., 2012; Li et al., 2015) especially with oxygen-containing groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl and epoxy groups which can greatly enhance their hydrophilia and biocompatibility. Therefore, GQDs based materials have significant potential for biosensor applications. Additionally, various metallic nanocomposite materials have been investigated for nonenzymatic amino acid biosensors (Guo et al., 2014; Heli et al., 2010; Kakhki and Shams, 2013). Traditionally, noble metals are the favored candidates for electrochemical biosensors due to their inherent electrocatalytic activities. For example, Devasenathipathy et al. and Silva et al. used gold nanoparticles and multi-walled carbon nanotubes as enhanced materials to build l-cys biosensors (Devasenathipathy et al., 2014; Fde et al., 2013). However, due to the high price and scarcity of noble metals, nanocomposite materials with great performances and low costs have been intensively explored to develop new functional nanomaterials.

Prussian Blue (PB), a classical prototype of mixed-valence transition metal hexacyanometalates, is a well-studied material for its excellent electrochemical and electrocatalytic properties (Fu et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2015) and has already been used for the electrocatalytic determination of l-cys (Qu et al., 2011). Nevertheless, there is still lot of room to improve the PB-based nanocomposite materials, in consideration of their particular limited stability and their high overpotential in l-cys oxidation (Pandey et al., 2012). Nanocomposites of PB and carbon nanotubes, graphene, graphene oxide and nitrobenzene-functionalized reduced graphene oxide have been investigated in recent years (Jin et al., 2010; Liu et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2010, 2013). However, to the best of our knowledge, little attention has been paid to the fabrication or application of PB and GQDs nanocomposite materials, which can bring many advantages for the electrocatalytic sensing of l-cys, such as simplicity, fast response, high sensitivity and low overpotential. Moreover, the cost of Prussian Blue promoted by GQDs is much lower than other common nanomaterials used for l-cys detection such as noble metals or their nanocomposites (Fde et al., 2013; Pandey et al., 2012).

In this work, we have developed a PB nanocomposite hybrid film anchored on a GQDs modified graphite felt (GF) electrode, via a spontaneous redox reaction between FeCl3 and K3[Fe(CN)]6. The reaction rate of the PB formation was obviously enhanced by the presence of GQDs. A polypyrrole (PPy) film was electrodeposited on the surface of the electrode to improve the electrochemical stability of the device. The as-prepared electrode exhibited remarkable electrochemical property for an efficient electrochemical determination of l-cys.

GF was obtained from commercially available polyacrylonitrile-based graphite fibers pretreated and activated previously described (Han et al., 2013). Pyrrole was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China) and purified by distillation. Other chemicals (analytical grade) were used as received without further purification.

A CHI 618 C Electrochemical Workstation (CH Instruments, Shanghai, China) was employed for all electrochemical experiments. A conventional three-electrode system was used, including a freestanding GF as working electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as reference electrode and a platinum wire electrode as counter electrode. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was performed on a JEOL JSM-6701F. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was carried out on a TecnaiTM G2 F30. UV–vis spectrophotometry was realized on an Agilent 8453 UV-spectrophotometer. Power X-ray diffraction (XRD) data was collected at a scan rate of 10°min−1 in a 2θ range of 10–80° with graphite monochromatized Cu Kα radiation (λ=0.15406nm) on a Rigaku D/MAX-2400 X-ray diffractometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was obtained using KBr pellets after baseline correction on a NICOLET NEXUS-670-FTIR spectrometer.

The GQDs solution was prepared following a bottom-up method with a little modification (Dong et al., 2012b). Briefly, 2g of citric acid was put into a 5mL round-bottom flask and heated at 200°C for 30min. The obtained orange liquid was added into 50mL of a 10mgmL−1 NaOH solution under vigorous stirring and neutralized to pH 7.0 with NaOH, an aqueous solution of GQDs was obtained.

The activated GF was put into a centrifuge tube with 1.4mL the above-mentioned GQDs solution and placed in an ultrasonic bath for 1h to get GQDs immobilization on GF, named as GQDs/GF. The GQDs/GF was then immersed in 20mL of a fresh aqueous solution of 1.0mmolL−1 of K3Fe(CN)6, 1.0mmolL−1 of FeCl3, 0.1molL−1 of KCl and 0.025mmolL−1 of HCl for 1h and then carefully washed by ultrapure water and dried at 60°C in a vacuum oven for 6h. The PB functionalized GQDs adsorbed on GF was referred as GQDs@PB/GF. For comparison, a bare GF was also treated in the same conditions, without GQDs, and was named as PB/GF.

The PPy/GQDs@PB/GF was synthesized by potentiostatic deposition of PPy onto the GQDs@PB/GF in 20mL of an aqueous solution containing 25mmolL−1 of pyrrole, 20mmolL−1 of NaClO4 and 28mmolL−1 of sodium dodecyl sulfate. The electropolymerization of PPy was performed at 0.8V (versus Ag/AgCl) for 10s. The resultant electrode, named as PPy/GQDs@PB/GF, was washed five times by ultrapure water and dried at 60°C in a vacuum oven overnight.

The fabrication process consisted of three simple steps, illustrated in Fig. S1. Firstly, GQDs fabricated by a bottom-up procedure based on the carbonization of citric acid – were adsorbed on the surface of activated GF in an ultrasonic bath (GQDs/GF). Then PB was grafted by a reaction between the precursors Fe3+ and [Fe(CN)6]3− in solution (GQDs@PB/GF). At last, a PPy film was potentiostatically deposited on the as prepared electrode (PPy/GQDs@PB/GF).

To verify the morphology and the composition of the PPy/GQDs@PB/GF samples, techniques such as SEM, TEM, FTIR and XRD were used to characterize the as-prepared samples. The micrograph of the initial GF substrate is presented in Fig. S2A and show a three-dimensional macroporous open-pore structure with fibers with diameters around 7–9μm. By comparing the SEM pictures of Fig. 1A (GQDs/GF) and Fig. 1A inset (only GF), no obvious morphological modification of the fibers was noticed before and after the treatment by GQDs at the micron scale, because the dimension of GQDs is too small to be surveyed by SEM. Indeed, the morphology of the GQDs was investigated by TEM (Fig. S3), in which GQDs with a uniform diameter distribution of less than 10nm. Without GQDs, only a few PB objects have been deposited on GF (Fig. 1B inset). After immersion of the GQDs/GF in the [Fe(CN)6]3−/Fe3+ solution for 1h, numerous PB was deposited on the surface of the GQDs/GF with some agglomeration (Fig. 1B). Looking at the TEM picture of GQDs@PB/GF (Fig. 1C), we can see the presence of GQDs on the surface of PB objects showing a good homogeneity of the material, without any phase segregation. We also observed a network of cracks throughout the film up to the GF substrate (Fig. S4), which was known to lower its stability (Koncki and Wolfbeis, 1998). To immobilize the PB, a PPy film was coated on the GQDs@PB/GF surface by potentiostatic deposition. The SEM picture of the as-obtained PPy/GQDs@PB/GF film demonstrated a much better quality with no visible cracks and lower agglomeration of PB (Fig. 1D). The deposition process may imply two different processes: a uniform PPy layer electropolymerization on the electrode surface and a disaggregation of the agglomerated PB particles. As a control, we deposited a PPy film separately on bare GF and found the presence of a porous film (Fig. 1D inset).

To demonstrate the enhancing effect of GQDs for the PB synthesis, we followed the absorbance of the reacting [Fe(CN)6]3−/Fe3+ solution by UV–vis spectrophotometry (Fig. 1E), where the peaks at 420nm and 710nm belong to the absorbance of [Fe(CN)6]3− and PB respectively. We measured the absorbance every minute with 2mL of 1.0mmolL−1 PB precursor solution in two cuvettes and 2μL GQDs solution addition in one of them after 60min, and found that the formation rate of PB was significantly increased after addition of GQDs (Fig. 1E inset). Recent investigations have demonstrated that graphene oxide, reduced-graphene oxide and carbon nanotubes can reduce Fe3+ to Fe2+, which reacts with [Fe(CN)6]3− immediately to form PB (Jin et al., 2010; Liu et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2010). As the structure of GQDs comes from a single layer or few layers of graphene, confined in a size of only some nanometers, we speculate that GQDs have a similar effect for the PB fabrication with the Fe3+ of the precursor solution.

The FTIR spectra of GQDs@PB/GF, PPy/GQDs@PB/GF and GF are reported in Fig. 1F. The absorption band at 2083cm−1 is a common characteristic of PB, corresponding to the stretching vibration of the CN groups (Qiu et al., 2007), and the absorption band at 503cm−1 is due to the formation of the M–CN–M′ bridge (Wilde et al., 1970), both indicating the formation of the PB coordination network. The absorption bands at 1615cm−1 and 3436cm−1 refer to the H–O–H bending mode and O–H stretching mode, respectively, which indicate the presence of interstitial water. The bands at 1628cm−1 and 1551cm−1 can be attributed to the fundamental vibration in the pyrrole ring, and the peak at 1298cm−1 and 1043cm−1 can be assigned to the C–H in plane vibration (Jin et al., 2012) (inset of Fig. 1F, enlarged view of trace b), suggesting the PPy film deposition successfully. The absorption bands around 1197cm−1 appeared in all traces and was due to the stretching vibration of C–O of GF (He et al., 2013).

The structures of the as-prepared GQDs@PB/GF, PPy/GQDs@PB/GF and GF were investigated by XRD, as shown in Fig. 1 G. The XRD patterns exhibited diffraction peaks close to 25.8°, 41.2° and 52.5°, which can be attributed to the diffraction of GF, while peaks near 22.1° and 45.5° referred to the (2 0 0) and (4 0 0) reflections of the face-centered-cubic phase of PB (JCPDS card No 73-0687) (Wang et al., 2012). The intensity of the PB peaks decreased after the deposition of PPy. This effect can follow the modification of the morphology observed by SEM (Fig. 1D), and a loss of the crystallinity of the PB objects during the electrodeposition.

Fig. 2A shows the cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of a PPy/GQDs@PB/GF, a GQDs@PB/GF and a PB/GF electrode between −0.2V and 0.5V at a scan rate of 50mVs−1 in 0.5molL−1 KCl solutions. A pair of distinct redox peaks at 0.21/0.15V for Fe(III)/Fe(II) was observed for the PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode, which is related to the Prussian White (PW)/PB reaction. The peak-to-peak separation (ΔE p) was only equal to 60mV and the ratio of the anodic to the cathodic currents (I pa/I pc) was ~1, indicating that a reversible process with a rapid electron transfer occurred at the electrode. In comparison, the potential separation of the GQDs@PB/GF electrode was two times larger (ΔE p=0.12V). The PPy film thus significantly improved the electron transfer rate. On the other hand, the peak currents of the GQDs@PB/GF electrode were higher, showing that some activate sites of the PB were covered up by the PPy film. The peak currents were weak at the PB/GF electrode, because the deposition amount of PB on GF surface was little in absence of GQDs (as shown in the SEM picture of Fig. 1B inset). Then, the electrochemical stability of the PPy/GQDs@PB/GF and GQDs@PB/GF electrode was studied by examining their CV response to 100 consecutive cycles (Fig. 2B and S4). The current of the GQDs@PB/GF electrode declined fast along with the cycles of CV (1.2mA after 100 cycles, Fig. S5), but the PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode showed an excellent stability, with a slight decrease of 0.3mA after 100 cycles, thanks to the PPy film (Fig. 2B).

Furthermore, in order to characterize the charge transport characteristics, the CVs of the PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode at an increasing scan rates from 20 to 1000mVs−1 were investigated (Fig. 2C). The results showed that the anodic and the cathodic peak currents were proportional to the square root of the scan rate ((Vs−1)1/2) between 20 and 1000mVs−1 (Fig. 2D), which is characteristic of a diffusion controlled redox process. Fig. 2E shows the variations of potentials E p–E° (E p is the peak potential value for the anodic and cathodic peak and E° is the formal potential taken as the average of the anodic and the cathodic peak potentials at a slow scan rate). The over-potentials were linearly dependent on the logarithm of scan rates when V>0.2Vs−1. Based on Eqs. (1) and (2) (Guo et al., 2014; Laviron, 1979), the electron transfer constant (k s), as well as the electron transfer coefficient (α) can be calculated: α=0.5, k s=2.6s−1,
(1)
α
/
(
1 −
α
)
=
v
a
/
v
c
(2)
k
s
=
nF
α
v
c
RT
=
nF
(
1 −
α
)
v
a
R

where
v
a
and
v
c
represent the critical scan rates obtained from the intercepts of the straight line fitting to the anodic and cathodic data. The value of k s was in good agreement of data reported previously for PB (Salazar et al., 2012), indicating that the electrode can efficiently promote electron transfer.

In order to investigate the efficiency of the PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode as an electrochemical sensor of l-cys, CVs were performed in the absence and presence of l-cys in a 0.5molL−1 KCl solution with a scan rate of 50mVs−1 ( Fig. 3A). A significant improvement of the anodic current of PW/PB occurred after the addition of of 2µL of a 1000μmolL−1 l-cys solution, which indicated the mediated oxidation of l-cys by the PW/PB redox couple. The reaction scheme for l-cys oxidation mechanism can be represented as follows (Pandey et al., 2012):
(3)
Fe III
[
Fe II
CN
6
] +
K +
+ RSH →
KFe II
[
Fe II
CN
6
] +
H +
+ RS •
(4)
KFe II
[
Fe II
CN
6
] →
Fe III
[
Fe II
CN
6
] +
K +
+
e −
(5)
2 RS· → RS −
SR

A potential of 0.22V was selected as the working potential for amperometric determination of l-cys using a PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode since the current reached a limiting value at approximately 0.22V. Fig. 3B presents a typical current-time response for successive 2μL additions of l-cys solution with increasing concentrations, in 20mL of a 0.5molL−1 KCl solution, under continuous stirring. As shown in Fig. 3C, the calibration curves for l-cys detection demonstrated that the sensitivity of PPy/GQDs@PB/GF was 0.41Amol−1 L in the l-cys concentration range of 0.2–50μmolL−1 and 0.15Amol−1 L in the l-cys concentration range of 50–1000μmolL−1. The detection limit (S/N=3) was found to be 0.15μmolL−1. For further assessing of our newly designed l-cys sensor, a comparison of its performance with those of other electrodes reported in the literature, and used for electrocatalysis of l-cys were listed in Table 1. It could be seen that the PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode exhibited remarkable results with a very low detection limit and a large liner range with a high sensitivity.

Additionally, the PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode had a highly stable amperometric response to a 10µmolL−1 l-cys solution recorded over a continuous period of 2000s. As illustrated in Fig. S6, the response remained nearly stable with less than 10% current diminutions after 2000s. The long-time stability and repeatability with the PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode was evaluated by examining the current response of a 10μmol L−1 L-cys solution addition every three days. The results are summarized in Fig. S7; the system kept 90% of the original current difference after 21 days, suggesting that the electrode has good stability for L-cys determination.

To investigate the anti-interference capability of the PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrodes for the detection of L-cys, the following interferents of some important biomolecules, such as L-methionine, DL-methionine, L-cystine, glutathione, N-acety-l-cysteine DL-cysteine, glucose, d-fructose, uric acid, glycine, l-proline and phenylalanine were examined during amperometric sensing of l-cys. Fig. 4 shows the relative response of PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode on the addition of 10μmolL−1 of l-cys followed by addition of 10μmolL−1 of each of L-methionine, DL-methionine, L-cystine, N-acety-l-cysteine, DL-cysteine, glucose, d-fructose, uric acid, glycine, l-proline and phenylalanine, and 2μmolL−1 of glutathione. No significant interference was observed, indicating high selectivity for l-cys of the sensor.

To validate the applicability of the present electrodes in practical analysis, the determination of l-cys in the sample dosage forms was examined by analyzing the commercially available l-cys capsule samples ( Table 2). It was found that the l-cys concentration determined by this method was in good agreement with the reported values and the PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode may have promising potential in practical applications.

In conclusion, we have successfully prepared a novel PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode with a simple synthetic method, at room temperature. This electrode shows an efficient detection of l-cys. Besides increasing the conductivity of the device, the GQDs are found to be of great importance for the PB formation. Owing to the great efficiency of PPy/GQDs@PB/GF electrode in L-cys determination, the electrode may have potential applications in medicine, food control or biological tissues analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support was provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Projects 20603014 and 21403098) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Project lzujbky-2011-116).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2015.10.088.
